claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 15 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 15 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 15 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children under the age of 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have one child, Alex, who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. And do you live with Alex in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me here in the UK. I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you. Are you the person responsible for Alex's day-to-day care - for example, providing food, clothes, and other needs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, that's actually a bit complicated. Alex has been in hospital for a while now - about three and a half months or so. I haven't really been able to afford to spend money on their needs while they're in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand, thank you for sharing that. That is an important detail. 

Can you tell me - during the time Alex has been in hospital (approximately 3.5 months), have you been regularly spending money on them, such as buying clothes, toys, books, or other items for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to. Like I said, I haven't been spending money on their needs while they've been in hospital.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Unfortunately, based on your circumstances, **you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

While you and Alex live together in the UK and Alex is only 8 years old (which would normally meet the basic eligibility requirements), Alex has been in hospital for approximately 3.5 months, which is around 14 weeks.

According to Child Benefit rules, if a child is in hospital for more than 12 weeks, you're usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit. There is an exception to this rule - you can continue to receive Child Benefit if you regularly spend money on your child while they're in hospital (such as buying clothes, toys, books, or other items for them).

However, since you've confirmed that you haven't been able to spend money on Alex's needs during their hospital stay, this exception doesn't apply in your situation.

I'm sorry this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If your circumstances change - for example, if you're able to start regularly spending money on Alex's needs - you may want to contact HMRC to discuss your situation.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK, which normally would meet the basic eligibility criteria. However, Alex has been in hospital for approximately 3.5 months (around 14 weeks), which exceeds the 12-week threshold stated in the rules. According to the Child Benefit rules for children in hospital, after 12 weeks in hospital, the claimant is usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit. There is an exception to this rule if the claimant regularly spends money on the child while they are in hospital. However, the claimant has explicitly stated that they have not been able to spend money on Alex's needs while in hospital. Therefore, the exception does not apply, and the claimant would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who has been in hospital for approximately 3.5 months. While the claimant and Alex live in the UK and would normally meet basic eligibility criteria, the extended hospital stay exceeds the 12-week limit. After 12 weeks in hospital, Child Benefit entitlement usually ends unless the claimant regularly spends money on the child. Since the claimant has confirmed they have not been able to spend money on Alex's needs during the hospital stay, they do not meet the exception criteria and are therefore not eligible for Child Benefit.
